metric_value: SD card predicted as contender but not assured winner in format war
notes: Aberdeen notes SD is one contender but not the assured winner since card type is not well understood by consumers

metric_value: SD became dominant memory card standard by 2010
notes: SD cards ultimately won the format war; CompactFlash retreated to professional niche, Memory Stick and xD discontinued

metric_value: Vast majority of participants own digital cameras
notes: Desktop computers, printers, and digital cameras are the most commonly owned digital technology

metric_value: USB cable is dominant photo transfer method
notes: Most respondents transfer photos via cable connection between digital camera and computer

metric_value: Minority of respondents use memory card readers
notes: A few respondents (particularly in San Diego) transfer photos by moving a memory card to a card reader

metric_value: Many respondents cannot identify their memory card brand
notes: Many respondents cannot say what brand of memory card they use; Sony Memory Stick users are most brand-aware

metric_value: Sony Memory Stick users most brand-aware among card users
notes: Sony Memory Stick users appear to be most brand-aware of any memory card format

metric_value: Memory cards used primarily in digital cameras and PDAs
notes: Respondents using memory cards most commonly report they use them in digital cameras and/or PDAs

metric_value: Most users never remove memory card from camera
notes: Most users of memory cards in digital cameras never remove their card since all transferring is done through a cable connection

metric_value: Size/portability, capacity choice, reusability, declining prices
notes: Focus groups reached consensus on memory card advantages: small size, capacity choice, reusability, and improving price points

metric_value: Small size (easy to lose), incompatible formats, price in larger capacities
notes: Disadvantages: small size can lead to loss; variety of incompatible formats; price particularly in larger capacities

metric_value: High cool factor; excitement about interchangeability
notes: Most frequent response to SD card demonstration was 'wow!'; participants visibly excited about content interchangeability

metric_value: Versatility/universality across devices is top perceived benefit
notes: The card's ability to be used with many different types of technologies sets it apart from competitors

metric_value: Need for compatible equipment is primary purchase barrier
notes: Key drawback: users need compatible equipment; most respondents currently own no compatible SD equipment

metric_value: Majority adopt wait-and-see attitude toward SD card purchase
notes: Many respondents will take a wait-and-see attitude; Boston respondents particularly concerned about long-term market acceptance

metric_value: 10-15% premium willingness for SD capability
notes: Many respondents willing to pay a premium for SD card capability in new equipment; quite a few indicate 10-15% range

metric_value: 256 MB to 1 GB preferred where price not a consideration
notes: After studying capacity chart, respondents strongly chose higher capacities, mostly 256 MB to 1 GB

metric_value: Most prefer one large card over several smaller ones
notes: Given a choice, consumers choose one big capacity card over multiple smaller cards for family use

metric_value: SD capability would serve as tie-breaker in product purchase decisions
notes: Vast majority see SD capability as minor factor but would opt for SD-capable product when choosing between similar products

metric_value: Once one SD product owned, capability sought in subsequent purchases
notes: Most respondents agree that having one SD-capable product would make them prioritize this capability in future purchases

metric_value: Decade-long transition predicted for full digital electronics adoption
notes: Aberdeen puts the transition to full memory card-based digital electronics at the better part of a decade to complete

metric_value: SD cards became ubiquitous by approximately 2010-2013
notes: The decade prediction was accurate: by 2010-2013 SD slots were standard in cameras, laptops, tablets, and smartphones

metric_value: Wide retail distribution expected across electronics and mass merchants
notes: Respondents expect SD cards at Best Buy, Circuit City, computer stores, office superstores, warehouse clubs, and mass merchants

metric_value: Circuit City went bankrupt in 2009
notes: Circuit City cited as expected retailer but filed for bankruptcy in 2008 and was liquidated in 2009

metric_value: In-store demonstrations recommended as primary marketing strategy
notes: Multiple focus group teams recommend in-store product demonstrations showing how memory card moves from product to product

metric_value: Size, portability, universality, capacity, ease-of-use, speed
notes: Teams across all focus groups consistently identified these six key benefits

metric_value: Wireless data transfer identified as ultimate goal by respondents
notes: A few respondents suggest wireless capability as the ultimate solution for digital data transfer problems

metric_value: Wireless transfer became ubiquitous via WiFi, Bluetooth, and cloud services
notes: By 2015+ WiFi Direct, Bluetooth, AirDrop, and cloud services largely supplanted physical media transfer for many use cases

metric_value: Digital cameras identified as primary driver of memory card awareness
notes: The explosive growth of digital cameras combined with ubiquitous home PCs and Internet access is a clear market stimulant

metric_value: Few respondents own MP3 players; music downloaded to PCs and burned to CDs
notes: Only a few respondents have MP3 players; majority download music to PCs and transfer to CDs

metric_value: PDAs used primarily for work/business; lower ownership than cameras/PCs
notes: More males than females use PDAs; primarily for schedules and directories; key application typically work-related

metric_value: CDs are primary long-term storage medium for photos and music
notes: Photos stored on hard drives with CD backup; music transferred to CDs to save hard drive space; CDs perceived as reliable and cheap

metric_value: VHS tapes dominant for video storage from camcorders
notes: Camcorder footage typically transferred to VHS tapes via cable connection; a few use CDs

metric_value: No respondents use memory cards for permanent storage
notes: No one participating in this research uses memory cards to store digital data permanently; cards considered too expensive and easy to lose

metric_value: Digital electronics market more mature than expected prior to study
notes: Aberdeen views the market as more mature from a receptivity and likely future buying pattern basis than conjectured prior to the study

metric_value: Standardizing on one format ensures compatibility across devices
notes: Consumers easily connected that standardizing on one memory card format ensures compatibility across a wide variety of digital products

metric_value: Respondents expect SD card prices to decline over time
notes: Overall respondents are prepared to pay less than anticipated prices and expect product prices to decline over time

metric_value: SD card prices declined dramatically; 1GB card went from ~$100 in 2003 to under $1 by 2020
notes: Consumer expectation of price decline was correct; memory card prices dropped exponentially over the following two decades

metric_value: Few respondents have home networks for data transfer
notes: Few respondents have established home networks and fewer still use them to actively transfer digital data

metric_value: Digital camcorders owned primarily by families with children
notes: Video camcorders most likely owned by families raising children; key motivation is chronicling child development

metric_value: Word-of-mouth is primary information source for digital technology
notes: Most frequently cited source of technology information is recommendations from friends, family, and co-workers

metric_value: Internet is second most important information source
notes: Many respondents go online to learn about new products or get additional information about products they may already use

metric_value: E-mail identified as key behavioral driver for digital photo adoption
notes: Digital photos are e-mailed to friends and family; e-mail is a key behavioral driver connecting digital cameras to broader digital adoption

metric_value: SD cards predicted to potentially replace CDs and videotapes for storage
notes: Some respondents particularly in San Diego feel SD Memory Card could replace other storage devices saving time and space

metric_value: SD cards partially replaced optical media; cloud storage became primary replacement
notes: SD cards replaced some optical media use cases but cloud storage and streaming became the primary replacements for CDs and tapes

metric_value: IHA commissioned research to develop effective SD card marketing strategies
notes: IHA commissioned primary qualitative market research to better understand target audience needs, expectations, and buying behaviors

metric_value: Results presented as hypotheses not facts; small non-representative samples
notes: Aberdeen cautions that results should be checked against quantitative data and/or verified by additional research

metric_value: Technology adoption within households driven by one tech-savvy member
notes: Typically one adult head-of-household plus teenaged/young adult children handle digital data transfer; simplification key to broader adoption

metric_value: Consumers uncertain which format will become industry standard
notes: Many express reluctance to acquire SD-compatible technology because they are unsure it will become the industry standard

metric_value: Sony eventually abandoned Memory Stick in favor of SD card support
notes: Sony gradually added SD card slots alongside Memory Stick and eventually discontinued Memory Stick in most product lines
